It does not auto-classify risk or generate referral or treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.6 — frequency, bilateral foot examination, and clinician risk stratification
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting age-appropriate review, bilateral skin and sensory examination, perfusion, ulceration, callus, infection or inflammation, deformity, gangrene, Charcot features, and clinician-entered risk classification without calculating it.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.10–1.3.13 — specialist assessment, reassessment, and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting completed foot protection assessment, vascular or biomechanical review, clinician-selected reassessment, information already provided, and an entered follow-up plan.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation
sources.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting suspected-drug identity, indication, dose-count or exposure duration, route, reaction description, severity, timing, examination, and structured allergy record. It does not diagnose drug allergy, stop a medicine, generate treatment, or make a referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.1 and Tables 1–3 — assessment, reaction patterns, and timing
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting history, examination, timing after exposure, erythema, urticaria, angioedema, respiratory or cardiovascular features, macules, papules, fixed eruption, fever, lymphadenopathy, mucosal erosion, vesicles, blistering, detachment, pustules, photosensitivity, and vasculitic features without asserting a diagnosis.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.9 — recording and sharing drug-allergy information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured recording of drug name, reaction signs, symptoms, severity, date and time, indication, number of doses or days before onset, route, status, and specialist-investigation results already available.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management
sources.version: NICE NG126; last updated and reviewed 2026-06-17
sources.population: Women, trans men, and non-binary people with pain, bleeding, or other complications in early pregnancy up to 13 completed weeks.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, emergency, gynaecology, and early pregnancy assessment services.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for early-pregnancy pain, bleeding, ectopic-pregnancy warning signs, ultrasound, and hCG assessment; partial for uncomplicated pregnancy symptoms and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Overview and recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.4 — early pregnancy assessment services
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines the up-to-13-week complication population and services offering specialist assessment, ultrasound, serum hCG review, and timely referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.12 — presentation, assessment and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists pain, amenorrhoea, bleeding, gastrointestinal symptoms, dizziness or syncope, shoulder-tip pain, urinary symptoms, tenderness, haemodynamic signs, pregnancy testing, and escalation criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.7.6 — ultrasound for pregnancy location and viability
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines clinician-performed transvaginal or selected transabdominal ultrasound and cautions against overinterpreting one early scan or LMP alone.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.8.1–1.8.10 — hCG in pregnancy of unknown location
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires symptoms to take priority over hCG, limits hCG interpretation to appropriate clinical use, and requires clear worsening-symptom access and follow-up.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Epilepsies in children, young people and adults
sources.population: Children, young people, and adults with suspected or established epilepsy.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, specialist, emergency, and ongoing epilepsy care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for epilepsy assessment, monitoring, information, emergency planning, and risk review; international only and local driving, referral, and prescribing rules require UAE review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.3.7 — seizure assessment, EEG, and imaging
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports seizure-event and witness history, physical examination, ECG or metabolic context, EEG review, MRI review, recurrence after remission, and specialist assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 2.1.1–2.1.11 — information, safety, and daily impact
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports trigger, adherence, side-effect, safety, driving, employment or education, reproductive-health, and care-plan discussions.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 4.1.1–4.5.11 — treatment, monitoring, and review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports seizure-control and adverse-effect review, clinician-led medication and interaction review, antiseizure medication levels when indicated, care review, and pregnancy-related specialist review without mapping a dose.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 7.1.1–7.3.1 — status, cluster, and prolonged seizure plans
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation of seizure clusters or convulsive emergencies and a clinician-agreed individual emergency management plan without mapping emergency medication or doses.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 10.1.1–10.2.2 — epilepsy-related death and SUDEP risk
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports seizure frequency, adherence, alcohol or drug use, uncontrolled seizures, abnormal neurological findings, and clinician-documented risk and safety discussion.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Falls: assessment and prevention in older people and in people 50 and over at higher risk
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE pathway adaptation
sources.population: People aged 65 years and over, and people aged 50 to 64 with one or more factors increasing fall risk.
sources.clinical_setting: Community settings including emergency departments and minor injury units, and hospital inpatient settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for asking details of a fall and for comprehensive clinician assessment of specified falls-risk domains in the covered age group. It does not cover every traumatic injury, younger adults or children, and it must not auto-score fall risk.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.5 — identifying people for falls assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports asking about the details of a fall when a covered person presents after one and explicitly rejects automatic prediction-tool use.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.3 — comprehensive falls assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of cardiovascular, cognition, delirium, diet/fluid, dizziness, footwear, function, gait/balance, hearing, long-term conditions, medicines, neurological, osteoporosis, continence and vision domains when relevant.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Faltering growth: recognition and management of faltering growth in children — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG75; current official recommendations page checked 2026-07-11
sources.population: Infants and children with weight loss or concerns about faltering growth.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and specialist pediatric care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for faltering-growth assessment and feeding history; partial for undifferentiated acute poor feeding and not directly validated for UAE practice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.2–1.2.14
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies weight and height measurement, clinical/developmental/social assessment, detailed feeding or eating history, observation, and assessment-directed investigations.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.10–1.2.12
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies suckling, bottle feeding, routines, environment, aversion, interaction, appetite, food types, and feeding-diary domains.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.27–1.2.32
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines growth monitoring and referral for underlying disorder, failure to respond, slow growth, rapid weight loss, severe undernutrition, safeguarding concern, or acute illness.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Fractures (non-complex): assessment and management — Recommendations
sources.population: Children and adults with non-complex fractures managed in emergency or orthopaedic services.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency departments and orthopaedic clinics.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected non-complex fracture assessment, documentation, imaging, weight-bearing, information, and follow-up contexts; partial for a generic fracture-follow-up workflow and not validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Overview; recommendations 1.1 and 1.2
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Covers non-complex fracture assessment and follow-up, repeated pain assessment, definitive X-ray reporting, Ottawa ankle and foot rules, and selected scaphoid imaging.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.10 — ongoing orthopaedic management
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports selected orthopaedic follow-up, stability review, weight-bearing, cast or fixation context, and return review for non-improving symptoms in specified fracture populations.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.6.12 — documentation, information, and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires structured fracture documentation, image and report continuity, honest information, questions, treatment expectations, rehabilitation, mobilisation, weight-bearing, management-plan communication, and documentation.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Generalised anxiety disorder and panic disorder in adults: management — Recommendations
sources.population: Adults with known or suspected generalised anxiety disorder or panic disorder.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and specialist mental-health care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult GAD identification, assessment, stepped care, risk, and clinician-led management; not exact for children, every anxiety subtype, or UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.2–1.2.11 — identification, assessment, education, and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports anxiety and worry history, duration, distress, functional impairment, comorbid depression or anxiety, substance use, medical and treatment history, education, and active monitoring.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.17–1.2.33 and 1.2.44–1.2.48 — clinician-led treatment and shared decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented discussion of psychological or medication options, patient preference, treatment information, monitoring, and referral or relapse planning without autonomous treatment output.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.37–1.2.39 — high risk and specialist assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting severe functional impairment, self-harm or suicide risk, substance misuse, self-neglect, treatment history, home and community support, family impact, referral, and collaborative care planning.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.5 — diagnosis, presentation, and exclusion of acute physical problems
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured adult panic-disorder diagnosis, history, comorbidity review, panic presentations, and proportionate investigation to exclude acute physical problems before primary-care follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.6–1.3.25 — clinician-led treatment and shared decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented discussion of psychological and medication options, patient preference, information, monitoring, self-help, referral, and follow-up without autonomous treatment output or doses.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.26–1.3.33 — reassessment, risk, and specialist care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports reassessment of prior treatment and concordance, substance use, comorbidity, function, social support, stressors, avoidance, comprehensive risk, side effects, efficacy, and a shared care plan.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
sources.population: Adults aged 18 years and over with dyspepsia, symptoms suggestive of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, or both.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, primary-care, investigation, review, and specialist referral.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected adult reflux and dyspepsia symptom, alarm, investigation, review, and clinician-discussed management fields; the legacy workflow has no adult-only filter and UAE pathways require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Scope and recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.2 and 1.4.1 — symptom profile and common care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports adult recurrent epigastric pain, heartburn, acid regurgitation, bloating, nausea or vomiting, associated precipitants, meal timing, and clinician-discussed lifestyle documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.4 — referral guidance for endoscopy
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports immediate specialist referral for significant acute gastrointestinal bleeding, medicine-cause review, prior endoscopy review, and further assessment when new alarm signs are present.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.2–1.5.2 — H. pylori context and review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed H. pylori testing context and annual review for people needing long-term dyspepsia management; medicine names and doses are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.6.1–1.6.16 and 1.11.1 — GORD management and specialist referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented reflux management, medication-plan discussion, review, and specialist referral where symptoms do not respond or remain unexplained; no dose or autonomous treatment text is mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Headaches in over 12s: diagnosis and management
sources.population: People aged 12 years and over with headache disorders, including migraine.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and specialist assessment and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for headache and migraine assessment in people aged 12 years and over; partial for adult-only DHA comparison and not directly validated for UAE pathways outside the DHA source.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — assessment, red flags, and headache diary
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports sudden onset, neurological deficit, fever, trauma, pattern change, unexplained vomiting, further investigation or referral, and structured headache-diary fields.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.7 — headache and migraine features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports headache location, quality, severity, duration, frequency, activity impact, nausea, vomiting, light or sound sensitivity, aura, menstrual relation, and medication-overuse review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.6 — diary, reassessment, referral, and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording headache frequency, duration, severity, intervention effectiveness, impact, and clinician-led further investigation or referral when red flags develop.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.10–1.3.33 — migraine treatment and prophylaxis review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation that an acute or preventive migraine plan was clinician-decided and that prophylaxis was reviewed; no medicine name or dose is mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG98; last updated and reviewed 2023-10-02; minor link update 2025-03
sources.population: Adults aged over 18 presenting with hearing difficulties or hearing loss.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, audiology, and secondary care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult hearing-difficulty assessment, red-flag referral, otoscopy, audiology, and hearing-support documentation; partial for children and UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — initial, sudden, and urgent assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports first-presentation hearing assessment, exclusion of wax or acute infection, audiology, sudden or rapid loss, unilateral neurological signs, otalgia, otorrhoea, and immunocompromise review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.5–1.3.2 — additional symptoms, ear findings, and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports unilateral or asymmetric loss, tinnitus, vertigo, persistent ear pain or discharge, abnormal outer-ear or tympanic-membrane findings, wax, and selected MRI referral contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.8.1 — audiology assessment, management, and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires full relevant history, hearing and communication needs, psychosocial and activity impact, otoscopy, pure-tone audiometry, tympanometry when indicated, personalised plans, hearing options, follow-up, questions, and support.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Heart valve disease presenting in adults: investigation and management
sources.population: Adults with suspected or established heart valve disease, including adults with a cardiac murmur.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, secondary, and specialist cardiovascular care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult murmur assessment, echocardiography, specialist referral, and valve-disease monitoring; not applicable to children and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.2 — murmur assessment and echocardiography
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports murmur nature, family and medical history, symptoms, peripheral signs, abnormal ECG, and clinician-led echocardiography review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.3–1.1.7 — urgent assessment and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports exertional syncope, severe angina or breathlessness, urgent specialist assessment, echocardiography, and referral after echocardiography.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.2 — monitoring without current intervention
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-arranged review and repeat echocardiographic monitoring for established valve disease.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Heavy menstrual bleeding: assessment and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG88; last updated 2021-05-24; last reviewed 2024-12-19
sources.population: Women of reproductive age with heavy menstrual bleeding, including selected persistent intermenstrual or irregular bleeding contexts.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and secondary care assessment of heavy menstrual bleeding.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for heavy menstrual bleeding and selected persistent irregular or intermenstrual bleeding contexts; partial for undifferentiated abnormal uterine bleeding and not directly validated for UAE practice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.5 — history and physical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies bleeding nature, persistent intermenstrual bleeding, pelvic pain or pressure, quality-of-life impact, comorbidities, prior treatment, and examination context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.6–1.2.10 — laboratory tests
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires full blood count, limits coagulation testing to relevant history, and states that ferritin, hormone, and thyroid testing are not routine without specific indications.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.18 — investigations for the cause of HMB
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Links history and examination to ultrasound or hysteroscopy selection and identifies persistent intermenstrual or irregular bleeding among endometrial-pathology risk contexts.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Hyperparathyroidism (primary): diagnosis, assessment and initial management
sources.population: People with features or laboratory findings that may indicate primary hyperparathyroidism and people with confirmed disease.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and secondary care diagnostic testing, assessment, referral, management and monitoring.
sources.applicability_note: Partial for a generic calcium-result review because the source is disease-specific. Exact only for selected symptom, albumin-adjusted calcium, PTH, renal, vitamin D, DXA and clinician-led follow-up documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.10 — albumin-adjusted calcium and PTH testing
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports thirst, polyuria, constipation, osteoporosis, fragility-fracture and renal-stone context; repeat albumin-adjusted calcium, concurrent PTH and specialist-advice documentation when clinician-selected.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.3 — secondary-care testing and assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed vitamin D, urine calcium, renal function, DXA and renal-tract imaging in a confirmed or probable primary-hyperparathyroidism context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.10–1.6.2 and monitoring table — follow-up and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-arranged calcium, PTH, renal-function, DXA and specialist monitoring with recommendation-specific qualifiers; no interval is auto-generated.

sources.population: Adults undergoing blood-pressure measurement, hypertension assessment, or monitoring, including adults with type 2 diabetes where specified.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1-1.1.8 - training, device maintenance, measurement technique, and postural assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports validated and maintained devices, standardized measurement conditions, appropriate cuff size, pulse review, and clinician-recorded postural measurements.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1-1.2.13 - clinic, ambulatory, and home blood-pressure assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting repeated clinic readings, ABPM or HBPM source and averages, result context, annual measurement in relevant diabetes care, and clinician review without automatically diagnosing hypertension.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1-1.3.3 - cardiovascular risk and target-organ assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led cardiovascular-risk discussion and review of urine, blood, retinal, and ECG assessments where actually performed or already ordered.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.15-1.4.24 - monitoring and annual review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinic, home, or ambulatory blood-pressure monitoring, symptoms, medication and lifestyle discussion, and an annual review without generating targets, treatment, or advice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1-1.5.3 - severe hypertension and same-day specialist review features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting measured severe blood pressure plus assessed retinal, neurological, chest-pain, heart-failure, kidney-injury, or phaeochromocytoma features as prompts, never as automatic conclusions.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.5–1.1.8 — postural hypotension symptoms and standing blood pressure
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting postural dizziness or falls context, lying or seated and standing blood pressure measurements, timing, symptoms, and clinician review without diagnosing orthostatic hypotension or changing treatment.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Irritable bowel syndrome in adults: diagnosis and management
sources.version: NICE CG61; last updated 2017-04-04; management update reviewed 2025-04-30
sources.population: Adults with suspected or confirmed irritable bowel syndrome.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary-care assessment, investigation, management, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected adult IBS symptom-profile, investigation, lifestyle-discussion, and follow-up fields; the legacy workflow does not enforce adult age or the required symptom duration and diagnostic criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1.1 and 1.1.1.4 — IBS symptom profile
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports at least 6 months of abdominal pain or discomfort, bloating, bowel-habit change, relation to defaecation or stool frequency or form, straining, urgency, incomplete evacuation, food association, mucus, and selected extra-intestinal symptoms.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.1.2 — red-flag assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires clinical assessment and examination for suspected-cancer features and inflammatory markers for inflammatory bowel disease, with referral when present; specific cancer-feature fields require their own exact source.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.2.1–1.1.2.2 — diagnostic tests
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports full blood count, ESR or plasma viscosity, CRP, and coeliac antibodies in people meeting IBS criteria and identifies tests not required solely to confirm IBS.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1.1–1.2.1.8 — dietary and lifestyle advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented lifestyle, activity, diet, symptom-trigger, and specialist dietary discussion; specific advice is not inserted automatically.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.2.5.1 — follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports agreed symptom-response-based follow-up, annual review, and investigation or referral when new red-flag symptoms emerge.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Impetigo: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE antimicrobial and referral adaptation
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with impetigo, including reassessment after treatment or recurrent disease.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, primary care, and hospital reassessment of impetigo.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting worsening, non-improvement, serious-illness features, previous antimicrobial exposure, microbiology already obtained, result review, and clinician-led referral context. Antibiotic names, doses, durations, and treatment choices are excluded.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.7–1.1.9 — advice and reassessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting rapid or significant worsening, failure to improve after treatment, alternative-diagnosis concern, serious illness or cellulitis concern, and prior antibiotic exposure.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.11–1.1.14 — microbiology and result review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording skin or nasal swab context, frequent recurrence, and microbiology results already reviewed; it does not generate a test or antimicrobial change.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.15–1.1.16 — referral and specialist-advice criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting serious illness, widespread disease with immunocompromise, bullous disease in infancy, recurrence, systemic illness, complication risk, and an already made referral or specialist discussion.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation
sources.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
sources.clinical_setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting bite or sting type, local inflammatory or allergic features, infection and systemic-reaction assessment, worsening, severe pain, immunocompromise, travel, and sensitive-site context. It does not generate treatment, antibiotics, antihistamines, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — assessment and advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting rapid-onset local reaction, redness, itch, type and severity, possible infection, systemic reaction, tick context, and worsening or systemic illness only when assessed.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.8–1.1.11 — reassessment, referral, and specialist-advice qualifiers
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting infection development, rapid worsening, systemic illness, severe disproportionate pain, previous systemic reaction, immunocompromise, mouth, throat or eye location, exotic exposure, fever, persistent lesions, and travel context.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Joint replacement (primary): hip, knee and shoulder — Recommendations
sources.population: Adults having primary elective hip, knee, or shoulder replacement.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, secondary, and tertiary services before and after elective joint replacement.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for primary elective joint-replacement information and rehabilitation; partial for a generic orthopaedic postoperative workflow and not validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — shared decisions and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation of postoperative pain, wound care, recovery, rehabilitation, return to work or activity, concerns, questions, and procedure-specific information.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.10.1–1.10.6 — postoperative rehabilitation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented mobility, activities of daily living, rehabilitation goals, self-directed or supervised rehabilitation, functional impairment, and physiotherapy or occupational-therapy follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.11.1 — long-term care and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports orthopaedic review for new or worsening pain, limp, or loss of function related to a joint replacement while noting the absence of a general fixed monitoring schedule.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Leg ulcer infection: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE wound, vascular, microbiology, and referral adaptation
sources.population: Adults with a leg ulcer, including possible or established infection and reassessment after treatment.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care, community wound care, and hospital referral assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting ulcer cause context, redness or swelling beyond the ulcer, warmth, increased pain, fever, worsening, systemic illness, disproportionate pain, comorbid risk, and reviewed microbiology. It does not diagnose infection or generate antibiotics, testing, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.3 — causes, colonisation, and infection signs
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting underlying-condition context and clinician-assessed redness or swelling beyond the ulcer, warmth, increased pain, fever, severity, complication risk, and previous antibiotic exposure.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.7–1.1.11 — reassessment and microbiology-result review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting rapid worsening, non-improvement, systemic illness, pain out of proportion, previous antibiotics, microbiology sampling already performed, and results already reviewed.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.12–1.1.13 — referral and specialist advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording clinician-identified serious illness, diabetes, immunosuppression, lymphangitis, spreading infection, or completed referral or specialist discussion; it does not create the referral.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Lower urinary tract symptoms in men: management
sources.version: NICE CG97; last updated 2015-06-03; last reviewed 2024-12-19
sources.population: Men aged 18 years and over with lower urinary tract symptoms, with initial, specialist, complication, and retention qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial and specialist assessment, conservative and medicine management, referral, and urinary-retention care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for adult male LUTS and urinary-retention documentation. The unrestricted legacy workflows do not encode sex or every complication qualifier; treatment, medicine choices, catheter care, surgery, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Initial assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports symptom and medicine history, abdominal and genital examination, digital rectal examination, frequency-volume context, urinalysis, qualified PSA discussion, qualified creatinine, conservative-advice documentation, and referral criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Specialist assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports specialist history and examination, urinary flow, post-void residual, and recommendation-qualified imaging or cystoscopy context.

exact_sections.heading: Treating urinary retention
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports acute or chronic urinary-retention context, palpable or percussable bladder, residual volume, creatinine, qualified imaging, and clinician-led follow-up or referral; catheter and medicine instructions are not mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Medicines adherence: involving patients in decisions about prescribed medicines and supporting adherence
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: People prescribed medicines and healthcare professionals who prescribe, dispense, or review medicines.
sources.clinical_setting: Shared medicine decisions, adherence assessment, support, and periodic medicine review.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for non-judgemental adherence history, missed-dose or medicine-taking behaviour documentation, beliefs, concerns, practical barriers, preferred support, and periodic review. It does not recommend a medicine, dose, change, or adherence intervention automatically.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.19–1.1.23 — knowledge, beliefs, and concerns about medicines
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the person’s understanding, perceived need, concerns, adverse-effect concerns, daily-routine fit, and questions during medicine review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.8 — non-judgemental adherence assessment and tailored support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting missed doses, stopping or restarting, intentional or practical barriers, and support preferences without generating an intervention.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.2 — periodic review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting periodic review of knowledge, concerns, need, and clinician prescribing decisions.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Medicines optimisation: the safe and effective use of medicines to enable the best possible outcomes
sources.version: NICE NG5; recommendations reviewed March 2019 with no new evidence affecting them
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people taking one or more medicines, with care-transfer, reconciliation, review, and polypharmacy qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Health and social care medicines communication, reconciliation, structured medication review, and shared decision-making.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected medicine-list, adherence, side-effect, monitoring, concern, and reconciliation documentation. It does not support autonomous medicine changes, doses, treatment, referral, fixed follow-up, or UAE service pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Medicines-related communication between care settings
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports complete and accurate medicine information, prescribed and non-prescribed medicines, allergies, timing, monitoring, and discussion at care transitions.

exact_sections.heading: Medicines reconciliation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines reconciliation as identifying an accurate current medicine list, comparing records, recognising discrepancies, documenting changes, and including over-the-counter or complementary medicines.

exact_sections.heading: Medication review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured review of all medicines, views, understanding, concerns, questions, adherence context, adverse-reaction or side-effect context, monitoring, and circumstances for seeking professional advice.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Melanoma: assessment and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE guideline NG14; last updated 27 July 2022
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE skin-cancer pathway adaptation
sources.population: People with a pigmented lesion assessed in secondary or tertiary care and people receiving follow-up after melanoma treatment.
sources.clinical_setting: Specialist dermatology and skin-cancer assessment and surveillance.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for clinician-performed dermoscopy, baseline photography, interval image comparison, full skin and regional-node examination, access to medical photography, and follow-up support. It does not diagnose melanoma or generate biopsy, imaging, treatment, referral, or follow-up intervals.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.3 — dermoscopy and baseline photography
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording trained-clinician dermoscopy, baseline clinical or dermoscopic photography, and interval comparison for a clinically atypical melanocytic lesion in specialist care.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.9.1–1.9.11 — follow-up support and examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting concerns, personalized follow-up context, clinician-performed full skin and regional lymph-node examination, dermoscopy, medical photography, and completed specialist follow-up without generating an interval or scan.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Menopause: identification and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG23; last updated and reviewed 2026-04-15
sources.population: Women, trans men, and non-binary people registered female at birth with menopause-associated symptoms.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and specialist care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for menopause symptom identification, selected test limitations, shared discussion, and review; international and partial for UAE prescribing and local referral practice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.6 — information and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists menstrual-cycle changes, vasomotor, genitourinary, mood, musculoskeletal, and sexual symptoms and their variable severity and duration.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.6 — identifying perimenopause and menopause
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines symptom-and-cycle-based identification in people aged 45 or over and limits laboratory and imaging tests, including FSH in specified contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.3 — discussing management options
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires benefit-risk discussion tailored to age, circumstances, risk factors, preferences, and repeated review when HRT is chosen.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.5–1.5.13 — genitourinary symptoms
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Covers clinician-discussed moisturiser, lubricant, and vaginal-treatment options and ongoing review for genitourinary symptoms.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.9.1–1.9.3 — reviewing treatment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines documented review at three months, annually thereafter, or earlier for ineffectiveness, adverse effects, or clinical need.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Neuropathic pain in adults: pharmacological management in non-specialist settings
sources.population: Adults with neuropathic pain managed in non-specialist settings.
sources.clinical_setting: Non-specialist assessment, treatment review, and specialist referral.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult neuropathic-pain review and treatment monitoring; partial for a generic neuropathy workflow and not directly validated for UAE prescribing or referral pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.7 — assessment, treatment planning, referral, and review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports pain severity, daily activity, sleep, participation, physical and psychological wellbeing, medicine response and adverse effects, coping, clinician-led treatment planning, early and regular review, and specialist referral without mapping medicine names or doses.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome and obesity hypoventilation syndrome in over 16s
sources.population: People aged over 16 years with suspected or diagnosed obstructive sleep apnoea/hypopnoea syndrome.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment, sleep-service referral, diagnostic testing, treatment discussion, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected adult OSAHS symptom, assessment, sleep-study, referral, driving-risk, treatment-discussion, and monitoring fields; not applicable to children and not directly validated for UAE sleep-service or driving pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — initial assessment for OSAHS
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports snoring, witnessed apnoeas, unrefreshing or fragmented sleep, waking headache, sleepiness or fatigue, nocturia, choking, cognitive symptoms, obesity context, Epworth Sleepiness Scale, and STOP-Bang, while prohibiting use of Epworth alone to determine referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.2 — prioritising sleep-service assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports referral information on assessment scores, functional sleepiness, comorbidities, occupational or driving risk, and oxygen saturation or blood gases if available.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.6 — diagnostic tests for OSAHS
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed respiratory polygraphy, home oximetry where appropriate, polysomnography in the specified context, and use of sleep-study results by clinicians to diagnose and grade OSAHS.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1 and 1.5.1–1.6.7 — lifestyle and treatment discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports tailored clinician discussion of weight, smoking, alcohol, sleep hygiene, and CPAP in recommendation-qualified diagnosed OSAHS contexts; no medicine, device setting, or autonomous treatment output is mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.9.1–1.9.11 — follow-up and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports tailored follow-up, CPAP review, annual review after optimisation, symptoms, Epworth score, vigilance and driving, AHI or ODI, adherence, treatment experience, sleep hygiene, and clinician-reviewed sleep-study follow-up.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Overweight and obesity management
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with overweight, obesity, or central adiposity, subject to section-specific age criteria.
sources.clinical_setting: Identification, sensitive discussion, assessment, behavioural intervention, referral, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected permission-sensitive adult weight discussion, measurements, context, previous attempts, goals, clinician-discussed intervention or referral, and follow-up fields. It does not support automatic advice, medicine selection, dosing, or UAE referral pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.9.1–1.9.17 — permission, measurements, and adult assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports avoiding diagnostic overshadowing, asking permission before weight discussion or measurements, and recording height, weight, BMI, waist or waist-to-height context and weight-related health risk.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.11.1–1.11.14 — results, context, intervention discussion, and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports comorbidity, social or mental-health context, diet or activity goals, previous attempts, experiences, barriers, preferences, clinician-agreed intervention, and preference-sensitive referral documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Behavioural intervention recommendations — goals, progress, feedback, and follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting personalised goals, progress, barriers, support, and clinician-arranged review without inserting automatic lifestyle instructions.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Pneumonia: diagnosis and management
sources.population: Children, young people, and adults with community- or hospital-acquired pneumonia, subject to recommendation-specific setting and age qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: First contact, primary care, hospital, community treatment, reassessment, and post-discharge follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected pneumonia recovery, reassessment, observations, and post-inpatient chest-radiograph fields; international guidance requires UAE clinician review and the legacy workflow does not enforce every age or discharge qualifier.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2 and 1.6.7 plus terms — clinical observations and signs
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-recorded temperature, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, mental status, eating or hydration context, and focal chest examination findings without asserting severity or diagnosis.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.10.1–1.10.4 — recovery and when to seek further advice
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports review of fever, cough, sputum, breathlessness, chest pain, fatigue or functional recovery, worsening or non-improving symptoms, systemic illness, and clinician-documented safety advice, with adult and child qualifiers preserved.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.11.1–1.11.8 — reassessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports reassessment when symptoms or signs do not improve or worsen, clinician-led referral, microbiology where specified, and CRP or procalcitonin review in the qualified hospital treatment-failure context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.12.1–1.12.3 — follow-up chest X-rays
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports no routine post-inpatient follow-up radiograph, consideration at 6 weeks for smoking, age over 50, persistent or deteriorating symptoms, or unexplained weight loss, and shared decision-making using recent imaging, comorbidity, frailty, prognosis, treatment options, and preferences.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Pressure ulcers: prevention and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE clinical guideline CG179; last reviewed 30 November 2018
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults, children, young people, infants, and neonates at risk of or with a pressure ulcer.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, emergency, secondary, and tertiary care pressure-risk and wound assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting pressure-ulcer risk factors, skin assessment, ulcer surface area, depth, undermining, category, photography, and repeated assessment. It does not generate a risk score, diagnosis, treatment, dressing, offloading, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.7 — adult risk and skin assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting mobility, previous or current pressure ulcer, nutritional status, cognition, sensory impairment, posture, deformity, skin integrity, colour change, heat, firmness, moisture, and pain only when assessed.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.3 and 1.5.1–1.5.3 — ulcer measurement and categorisation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting pressure-ulcer surface area, validated measurement or photography, estimated depth, undermining, clinician-assigned category, and repeat assessment without automatically calculating or assigning a category.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Psoriasis: assessment and management
sources.population: Children, young people, and adults with psoriasis, including skin, nail, joint, severity, impact, and referral qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and specialist assessment and management of psoriasis.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for psoriasis identity, distribution, extent, nail involvement, high-impact sites, symptom and life impact, systemic upset, and joint-symptom assessment. Treatment and referral decisions are not mapped, and UAE adaptation remains required.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1.1–1.2.1.12 — assessment, severity, sites, and impact
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting psoriasis type, duration, extent, distribution, nails, high-impact sites, symptoms, systemic upset, daily-life impact, and wellbeing without inferring an individual diagnosis or treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.2.1–1.2.2.4 — psoriatic arthritis assessment and referral qualifiers
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports asking about joint symptoms and recording clinician-led specialist assessment or referral only when completed or arranged.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Renal and ureteric stones: assessment and management
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with suspected or confirmed renal or ureteric stones, with age and pregnancy qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Assessment, imaging, procedural management, metabolic testing, prevention, and follow-up of renal and ureteric stones.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for selected stone identity, imaging, stone-analysis, metabolic-workup, hydration-context, and clinician-discussed prevention documentation. Acute symptom assessment, treatment, procedures, fixed follow-up, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnostic imaging
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed CT or ultrasound imaging with age and pregnancy qualifiers.

exact_sections.heading: Metabolic testing
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports stone analysis, serum-calcium context, and qualified paediatric specialist metabolic assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Preventing recurrence
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting hydration or dietary context and prevention advice only when discussed by the clinician; numeric instructions are not mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Self-harm: assessment, management and preventing recurrence
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE legal and pathway adaptation
sources.population: Children, young people and adults who have self-harmed, unless a recommendation is age-specific.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, emergency department, hospital and mental-health care following self-harm.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for compassionate psychosocial assessment, emergency-department initial assessment, immediate safety, safeguarding, willingness for physical and mental care, care-plan status, observation and discharge documentation after self-harm. It does not apply a risk score, predict suicide or generate treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.5.17 — psychosocial assessment and care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording the person’s needs, concerns, strengths, vulnerabilities, context and clinician-performed psychosocial assessment without using a prediction score.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.7.12–1.7.20 — emergency-department initial assessment and care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting injury severity, emotional and mental state, distress, immediate safety, safeguarding, willingness for care, observation and care-plan status in the ED.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.2 and 1.9.6 — safeguarding and discharge
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording safeguarding consideration and clinician-confirmed assessment, plan, communication and follow-up before discharge after self-harm.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Stable angina: management
sources.version: NICE clinical guideline CG126, last updated 25 August 2016
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults with a clinician-established diagnosis of stable angina.
sources.clinical_setting: Outpatient stable-angina information, treatment-response review, and clinician-led investigation or revascularisation discussion.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting established stable-angina symptom course, daily impact, concerns, treatment response and adverse effects, and clinician-entered follow-up. It does not diagnose angina, infer stability, recommend a medicine or dose, or determine revascularisation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.7 — information, symptom course, daily impact, concerns, and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting established stable-angina symptom context, provoking factors, daily impact, expectations, sudden worsening already reported, and clinician discussion.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.3–1.4.5 — medicine information, adverse effects, and response review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting an already prescribed treatment, adherence discussion, adverse effects, and clinician review of response; no medicine or dose is generated.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.6–1.5.12 — clinician-led revascularisation discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording a completed clinician discussion of relative risks, benefits, coronary anatomy, comorbidities, and preferences without recommending CABG or PCI.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Stroke rehabilitation in adults
sources.population: Adults requiring rehabilitation after stroke.
sources.clinical_setting: Hospital, community, and long-term stroke rehabilitation and review.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult stroke rehabilitation assessment and longer-term review; not an exact source for isolated TIA follow-up and not directly validated for UAE services.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.14 — stroke assessment and rehabilitation plan
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports cognition, vision, hearing, swallowing, movement, continence, communication, nutrition, strength, sensation, balance, mood and mental health, pain, activity, participation, environment, medicines, allergies, goals, discharge information, and documented follow-up planning.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.17, 1.4.3, and 1.17.1–1.17.5 — transfer and long-term review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports post-transfer follow-up, information needs, daily activities, participation goals, work, driving and transport discussion, and review of health and social care needs at 6 months and annually.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Stroke and transient ischaemic attack in over 16s: diagnosis and initial management
sources.population: People aged over 16 years with suspected or confirmed stroke or transient ischaemic attack.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment, specialist TIA assessment, imaging, and secondary-prevention planning.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for TIA specialist assessment, imaging, and secondary-prevention documentation; partial for a broad long-term stroke or TIA follow-up workflow and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.7 — secondary prevention after confirmed TIA
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led secondary-prevention planning after specialist confirmation of TIA without mapping medicine names or doses.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.4 — brain and carotid imaging after specialist TIA assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed MRI and carotid imaging in the specified specialist-assessment context.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Surgical site infections: prevention and treatment — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG125; last updated 2020-08-19; last reviewed 2023-05-31
sources.population: Adults, young people, and children having a surgical procedure involving a skin incision.
sources.clinical_setting: Perioperative, postoperative, community, and specialist wound-care services.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for postoperative wound-care and surgical-site-infection documentation; partial for traumatic wounds, suture removal, and UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.4 — information, wound care, and infection recognition
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented postoperative wound-care information, infection-warning discussion, and who to contact if concern develops.

exact_sections.heading: Overview and recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.8
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines the postoperative surgical-wound population and supports wound dressing, cleansing, wound-healing, and structured postoperative wound-care documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.9–1.4.11 and surgical-site-infection definition
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports review of suspected infection, cellulitis, microbiology context, structured wound care, and local heat, redness, pain, swelling, discharge, separation, abscess, fever, or systemic signs.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Suspected cancer: recognition and referral
sources.population: Children, young people, and adults with symptoms or signs that may indicate cancer, subject to recommendation-specific age criteria.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care recognition, safety netting, investigation, and referral.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected suspicious skin-lesion, safety-netting, and age- or risk-qualified respiratory recommendations; international suspected-cancer pathways require UAE clinician review.
