exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.7.1–1.7.7 — skin cancers
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists suspicious pigmented-lesion features including size change, irregular shape or colour, diameter, inflammation, oozing and sensation change, plus dermoscopy and referral contexts for melanoma, squamous-cell carcinoma, and basal-cell carcinoma.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.14.6–1.15.2 — information and safety netting
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting warning symptoms, when to return for re-evaluation, agreed review, and patient-initiated review if symptoms recur, persist, worsen, or cause concern.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.6 — lung and pleural cancer respiratory features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician recognition of unexplained haemoptysis and age- and smoking-context combinations involving unexplained cough, fatigue, breathlessness, chest pain, weight loss, appetite loss, recurrent chest infection, clubbing, lymphadenopathy, or chest signs.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.7 — oesophageal, stomach, and pancreatic cancer features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician recognition of dysphagia, weight-loss combinations, and jaundice in recommendation-specific age and symptom contexts; it does not assert cancer or an automatic referral decision.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.5 — colorectal cancer features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician recognition of rectal bleeding, change in bowel habit, abdominal pain, weight loss, anaemia, and rectal-mass contexts with recommendation-specific age qualifiers; it does not assert cancer.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Suspected neurological conditions: recognition and referral
sources.population: Adults aged over 16 years with suspected neurological conditions.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment and referral from primary or community care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected adult dizziness, vertigo, limb weakness, sensory symptoms, and tremor referral features; not applicable to children and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.7 — dizziness and vertigo in adults
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports focal neurological and hearing red flags, acute vestibular syndrome, HINTS by trained clinicians, neuroimaging referral, and Hallpike assessment for positional vertigo.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.7.1–1.7.10 and 1.10.1–1.10.6 — limb weakness and sensory symptoms
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports sudden, progressive, fluctuating, unilateral, symmetrical, sensory-associated, and back-pain-associated weakness, urgent referral, and clinician-led imaging where specified.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.10.1–1.10.13 — sensory symptoms including tingling or numbness in adults
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports sudden unilateral numbness, rapidly progressive symmetrical sensory change with weakness or imbalance, recurrent sensory episodes, distal sensory distribution, reflex and gait context, selected metabolic tests, radicular symptoms, bowel or bladder context, and clinician-led referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.15.1–1.15.4 — tremor in adults
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports rest or asymmetric tremor, stiffness, slowness, balance or gait difficulty, symmetrical postural tremor, medicine, thyroid and alcohol review, functional impact, head tremor, and clinician-led referral.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Suspected sepsis in people aged 16 or over: recognition, assessment and early management
sources.population: People aged 16 years and over with suspected sepsis, excluding people who are or have recently been pregnant.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, ambulance, acute hospital, and emergency assessment settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for suspected sepsis recognition and assessment in non-pregnant people aged 16 years and over; partial for a broad fever or suspected-infection workflow and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1–1.2 — recognition, suspected infection, source, and risk factors
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports suspected-infection and fever presentation documentation, source symptoms, risk factors, and recognition that sepsis can present with nonspecific illness.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.3 — history, observations, circulation, respiration, skin, urine output, and source examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, consciousness, oxygen saturation, skin signs, urine output, fever or rigors, and examination for the suspected infection source.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5–1.6 — community and acute risk criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports confusion, respiratory compromise, hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria, temperature abnormality, non-blanching or mottled skin, and immunosuppression as assessed risk features.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.7 — emergency transfer, monitoring, and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports emergency transfer or escalation for high-risk presentations and documented monitoring, warning signs, and how to seek care for lower-risk presentations.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.8 and 1.11 — source identification and investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected blood and microbiology testing, urinalysis, chest radiography, and abdominal or pelvic imaging to identify an infection source.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Thyroid disease: assessment and management
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with suspected or diagnosed thyroid disease, subject to recommendation-specific age and disease qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Assessment, investigation, treatment discussion, monitoring, and follow-up of thyroid disease.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected thyroid-testing, thyroid-enlargement warning, hypothyroidism-monitoring, and hyperthyroidism-monitoring documentation. Symptom diagnosis, medicine selection or dose, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.6 — information and thyroid-enlargement warning symptoms
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports shared decision documentation, thyroid-medicine interaction information, and specific thyroid-enlargement warning symptoms including shortness of breath, rapid growth, hoarse voice, and swallowing difficulty.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.3.2 — suspected dysfunction testing and antibodies
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician suspicion context, mood or anxiety context, TSH, FT4, FT3, biotin history, repeat testing when symptoms change, and TPO antibody testing in recommendation-qualified cases.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.6 — follow-up and monitoring of primary hypothyroidism
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed TSH and FT4 monitoring, persistent-symptom review, and clinician-led follow-up for confirmed primary hypothyroidism without mapping a dose or autonomous treatment change.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.7.1–1.8.5 — follow-up and monitoring of hyperthyroidism
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed TSH, FT4, and FT3 monitoring, treatment-context review, and clinician-led follow-up or specialist advice without mapping medicine doses or autonomous treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.9.1–1.9.8 — thyroid enlargement with normal thyroid function
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of palpable thyroid enlargement or focal nodularity, recommendation-qualified ultrasound, compression symptoms, and clinician-led specialist assessment.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Tinnitus: assessment and management — Recommendations
sources.version: NICE NG155; published 2020-03-11; current official recommendations
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with tinnitus.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and secondary care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for tinnitus assessment, impact, referral, investigation, and support; not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.5 — experience, impact, support, and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports tinnitus experience, duration, concerns, noise and stress context, hearing association, sleep impact, management discussion, individual preferences, information, and questions.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.7 — urgent and non-urgent referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports review of suicide risk, neurological or vestibular symptoms, sudden hearing loss, distress, objective, unilateral, asymmetric, and pulsatile tinnitus, and clinician-arranged referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.10 — functional, sleep, and psychological impact
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation of quality-of-life, sleep, concentration, mental-health, behavioural, and psychological impact.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.1–1.4.9 — audiology, tympanometry, and imaging
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports audiological assessment, selected tympanometry, neurological or otological context, and differentiated imaging review for pulsatile, unilateral, asymmetric, or otherwise concerning tinnitus.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Transient loss of consciousness ('blackouts') in over 16s
sources.population: Adults and young people aged 16 years and older with suspected transient loss of consciousness.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment, emergency transfer, referral and specialist evaluation of transient loss of consciousness.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected event-history, witness-account, vital-sign, cardiovascular or neurological assessment, ECG-result, urgent-risk and transfer-of-record documentation. It is international guidance and does not establish the diagnosis or UAE disposition.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1.1–1.1.2.1 — initial history, witness account, examination, and vital signs
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports event and witness history, posture, prodrome, appearance, movements, tongue biting, injury, duration, confusion, focal weakness, previous events, medical and family history, medicines, vital signs, lying and standing blood pressure, cardiovascular examination, and neurological signs.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.2.2–1.1.2.4 — 12-lead ECG and additional tests
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-performed or reviewed 12-lead ECG and selected additional tests such as glucose or haemoglobin when indicated by the assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.4.1–1.1.4.6 — red flags and urgent cardiovascular assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urgent cardiovascular assessment for ECG abnormality, heart failure, transient loss of consciousness during exertion, family history of sudden cardiac death, new or unexplained breathlessness, or a heart murmur.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2 and 1.3 — postural hypotension and specialist cardiovascular assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led orthostatic assessment, repeat or previous ECG review, ambulatory ECG selection, further investigation, and specialist cardiovascular assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.1–1.5.4 — information, safety, driving, and recurrence
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented discussion of the event, tests, driving, safety, and what to do if another event occurs.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1.1–1.1.1.4 — event and witness history
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording what occurred before, during and after the event, posture, prodrome, appearance, movements, injury, duration, confusion and focal weakness from patient and witness accounts.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.2.1–1.1.2.4 — history, examination, vital signs, ECG and selected tests
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports prior-event, medical, family and medicine history, measured vital signs, cardiovascular and neurological signs, ECG status and clinician-selected testing when an underlying problem is suspected.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.1.3.1 — recording and transfer of records
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports careful recording of all accounts and transfer of paramedic, ECG and patient-report records to the receiving clinician.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.4.1–1.1.4.2 — urgent assessment features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-recorded urgent-risk context including ECG abnormalities, heart failure signs, exertional events, family history, unexplained breathlessness and murmur without generating urgency or referral.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse in women: management
sources.version: NICE NG123; minor links and language updates through March 2025
sources.population: Women aged 18 and over with urinary incontinence or overactive-bladder symptoms, with UTI, voiding, referral, and treatment qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial assessment, urine testing, bladder diaries, conservative management, and specialist referral.
sources.applicability_note: Partial for a sex-unspecified frequency or urgency workflow. Exact only for selected female urinary-incontinence or overactive-bladder assessment and clinician-documented conservative-management fields; medicines, procedures, fixed follow-up, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Assessing urinary incontinence — history and physical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urgency-incontinence or overactive-bladder symptom context, relevant contributing factors, and clinician-performed pelvic-floor assessment in adult women.

exact_sections.heading: Urine testing, residual urine, and bladder diaries
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urine dipstick, qualified culture, post-void residual, and bladder-diary documentation in adult women.

exact_sections.heading: Indications for referral to a specialist service
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led referral documentation for qualified bladder, urethral, voiding, neurological, haematuria, or recurrent unexplained UTI contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Lifestyle and behavioural interventions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting caffeine or fluid discussion and bladder training only when discussed or arranged by the clinician for qualified adult women.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Varicose veins: diagnosis and management — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults with varicose veins, lower-limb symptoms, skin changes, venous ulceration, or venous insufficiency context.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care and vascular-service recognition and referral assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for lower-limb pain, aching, discomfort, swelling, heaviness, itching, pigmentation, eczema, and chronic-venous-insufficiency context. It does not diagnose stasis dermatitis or venous eczema and does not generate referral or treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.2.2 — symptomatic veins and lower-limb skin changes
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting lower-limb pain, aching, discomfort, swelling, heaviness, itching, pigmentation, and eczema considered by the clinician in chronic venous insufficiency context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.2.2 — venous leg-ulcer history and referral context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting a current or healed venous leg ulcer and an already made vascular referral without generating diagnosis, referral, urgency, or treatment.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Venous thromboembolic diseases: diagnosis, management and thrombophilia testing
sources.version: NICE guideline NG158, last updated 2 August 2023
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: People presenting with signs or symptoms of deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism and people receiving related follow-up.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial medical assessment and clinician-led diagnostic pathways for suspected venous thromboembolic disease.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting a swollen or painful leg history, physical examination, conditional DVT or PE risk features, and clinician-entered assessment context. It does not calculate Wells or PERC scores, infer DVT or PE, order tests, prescribe anticoagulation, or determine urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.1–1.1.2 and Table 1 — DVT history, examination, and clinician-scored features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting swollen or painful leg context, general medical history, physical examination, laterality and swelling or tenderness findings, immobility, surgery, malignancy, previous DVT, and a clinician-entered score only. No score is calculated.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1.15–1.1.17 and Table 2 — PE history, examination, and clinician-scored features
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting chest pain, shortness of breath, haemoptysis, vital-sign and examination context, prior VTE, immobilisation or surgery, malignancy, and a clinician-entered score only. No diagnosis or score is inferred.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.2.4 — clinician-agreed monitoring and follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting an already agreed monitoring or follow-up plan and information already provided for confirmed or suspected low-risk PE; it does not generate that plan.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Violence and aggression: short-term management in mental health, health and community settings
sources.population: Adults, children and young people at risk of or involved in violence or aggression, with age- and setting-specific recommendations.
sources.clinical_setting: Mental health, general health, community and emergency-department settings, including post-incident review.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected objective risk-context, de-escalation, restrictive-intervention monitoring and post-incident documentation. It does not authorise restraint, tranquillisation, seclusion or any medicine and requires UAE legal and facility adaptation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.8–1.2.14 and 1.3.12–1.3.20 — risk assessment and de-escalation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports objective multidisciplinary risk assessment, prior incident and trigger context, service-user involvement, previously effective de-escalation and transfer of an existing risk assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.23–1.4.45 — restrictive-intervention safety and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting airway, breathing, circulation, vital signs, physical and psychological monitoring and clinician review only when a restrictive intervention actually occurred. Medicine instructions and doses are excluded.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.4.53–1.4.63 — post-incident debrief, documentation and review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording harm, ongoing risk, emotional impact, contributing factors, patient and witness perspectives, completed documentation, handover and formal review after an incident.

sources.issuing_organisation: Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne; Paediatric Improvement Collaborative
sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Abdominal pain — acute
sources.population: Children and adolescents with acute abdominal pain.
sources.clinical_setting: Hospital and emergency pediatric assessment; hosted by the Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne and PIC endorsed.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for acute pediatric abdominal pain but partial for general outpatient use, chronic pain, and UAE practice.

exact_sections.heading: Key points and background
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines acute pediatric abdominal pain as a common nonspecific presentation requiring assessment for urgent causes and red flags.

exact_sections.heading: Assessment — history
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies pain characteristics, systemic symptoms, diet, stool, vomiting, urinary, adolescent, psychosocial, and infant presentation history.

exact_sections.heading: Assessment — examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies appearance and movement, vital signs, hydration, abdominal inspection, tenderness, guarding, rigidity, masses, and serial examination.

exact_sections.heading: Management — investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: States that most children need no investigations and lists targeted urine, blood, and imaging investigations selected by the likely cause.

exact_sections.heading: Consultation, transfer, and discharge
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists severe pain, peritonism, bilious vomiting, suspected surgical disease, admission, follow-up, and parent advice contexts.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: Selected practice recommendations for contraceptive use, fourth edition
sources.population: People seeking initiation, continuation, switching, or support for contraceptive method use after medical eligibility is established.
sources.clinical_setting: Contraceptive counselling and service delivery.
sources.applicability_note: Global exact guidance for informed contraceptive choice and practical method use; partial for UAE-specific law, prescribing, and full medical-eligibility assessment, which requires the companion MEC and local review.

exact_sections.heading: 1.1 Purpose; 1.2 Scope; 1.3 Target audience
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines the guideline as practical contraceptive-use guidance for providers once medical eligibility has been established.

exact_sections.heading: 1.5 Contraceptive choice and informed consent
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires understandable information, voluntary informed choice, client preference, consent, and freedom from coercion.

exact_sections.heading: 1.6 Quality of care and access to products
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies method information on effectiveness, correct use, common effects, risks and benefits, return-to-clinic signs, fertility, STI protection, and opportunities for questions.

exact_sections.heading: 3.1 Examinations and tests; 3.2 Contraceptive eligibility; 5.1 Reasonable certainty that a woman is not pregnant
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines method-specific examination and test classification, preserves medical eligibility as a separate assessment, and supports pregnancy-status review before method initiation.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization and International Committee of the Red Cross
sources.exact_document_title: Basic emergency care: approach to the acutely ill and injured
sources.population: Adults and children with acute illness or injury presenting to first-contact emergency care.
sources.clinical_setting: First-contact emergency units and resource-limited emergency-care settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for structured trauma history, secondary survey, extremity perfusion, and open-wound assessment; partial for minor outpatient injury follow-up, imaging, closure, antibiotics, and UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: SAMPLE history and emergency disposition
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports signs and symptoms, allergies, medicines, past history, last intake, events or mechanism, examination, interventions, reassessment, and disposition documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Trauma secondary survey
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports inspection for wounds, bruising, swelling, tenderness, deformity, range of movement, neurological findings, severe pain, and other injury signs.

exact_sections.heading: Extremity trauma and perfusion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports deformity, distal pulse, capillary refill, sensation, neurovascular compromise, open fracture, splinting, and perfusion reassessment documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Open wound and laceration assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports laceration or abrasion, location, size and depth, bleeding, contamination, foreign-body concern, underlying structures, distal perfusion, dressing, splinting, tetanus, and return or disposition documentation.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: Guideline for the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of infertility
sources.population: Individuals and couples being evaluated and managed for infertility.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, reproductive-health, fertility, and specialist services adaptable to national settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact global guidance for infertility history, focused examination, diagnostic evaluation, and person-centred support; requires UAE adaptation and does not directly cover recurrent pregnancy loss as a separate presentation.

exact_sections.heading: Chapter 3.1–3.3 — objective, indication, initial consultation, and medical history
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports concurrent female and male evaluation, duration trying to conceive, menstrual, obstetric, gynaecologic, medical, surgical, medication, lifestyle, sexual, STI, partner, and prior-investigation history.

exact_sections.heading: Chapter 3.3 — focused examination and diagnostic evaluation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Specifies focused female examination including vital signs, BMI, thyroid and pelvic assessment and a clinically selected evaluation including semen, ovulation, tubal, and uterine assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Chapter 3.3 — diagnosis discussion, psychosocial support, and shared decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires preference-sensitive discussion, psychosocial support, transparent information, and consent-based collaborative decisions.

exact_sections.heading: Chapter 5.1–5.3 — female, male, and unexplained infertility diagnostic pathways
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines female, male, and unexplained-factor evaluation, menstrual-pattern context, ovulation confirmation, reproductive hormones, semen analysis, tubal assessment, and uterine-cavity assessment.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: Health care for women subjected to intimate partner violence or sexual violence: a clinical handbook
sources.jurisdiction: International guidance requiring UAE legal, safeguarding and referral adaptation
sources.population: Women subjected to intimate partner violence or sexual violence; the handbook does not directly address people under 18 or men.
sources.clinical_setting: Health-care encounters including first-line support, history, examination and documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for survivor-centred first-line support, consent to documentation, recording complaints, symptoms, signs and injuries, and history or examination only when clinically performed. It is not authority for universal screening or automatic reporting in the UAE.

exact_sections.heading: First-line support and LIVES
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports listening, documenting needs and concerns, validating, assessing safety and recording support discussed without asserting abuse or imposing action.

exact_sections.heading: Documenting partner violence
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports asking permission to document and recording complaints, symptoms, signs, injuries and relevant history in the person’s words without interpretation.

exact_sections.heading: History and physical examination after sexual violence
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports respectful, consent-based history and head-to-toe examination documentation only when relevant and performed; sexual-assault care is not generalized to every domestic-violence presentation.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: Scabies
sources.population: People with possible scabies or close contact with an affected person, including children, older people, and immunosuppressed people.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, primary care, outbreak, institutional, and dermatology assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for contact context, symptom timing, itch, burrows, papules, distribution, pediatric patterns, immunosuppression, crusted disease, and secondary infection documentation. It is not used to diagnose scabies or generate treatment or contact instructions.

exact_sections.heading: Symptoms and population considerations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting itch, night worsening, burrows, papules, site distribution, infant or child distribution, immunosuppression, crusting, and secondary skin infection context.

exact_sections.heading: Transmission and diagnosis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting close skin contact, household context, exposure before symptoms, characteristic distribution, and clinician-completed visual, dermoscopic, or microscopy assessment without inferring diagnosis.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: Guidelines for the management of conditions specifically related to stress
sources.population: Children, adolescents, and adults with symptoms and disorders specifically related to traumatic stress, bereavement, or acute stress.
sources.clinical_setting: Non-specialist and specialist health-care settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for trauma-related stress conditions and physical-cause exclusion; not exact for generic work stress, burnout, or every adjustment presentation, and UAE pathways require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Assessment before applying stress-specific recommendations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires full assessment of presenting mental-health problems and exclusion of physical causes before stress-specific management.

exact_sections.heading: Acute traumatic stress symptoms in the first month after a potentially traumatic event
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines acute traumatic stress through intrusion, avoidance, hyperarousal, and significant functional impairment after a potentially traumatic event and supports clinician-led psychological-first-aid or trauma-focused care documentation without medication invention.

sources.issuing_organisation: World Health Organization
sources.exact_document_title: WHO Guidelines for Safe Surgery 2009: Safe Surgery Saves Lives — Surgical Safety Checklist and Implementation Manual
sources.version: WHO 2009 surgical safety checklist and implementation manual
sources.population: Patients undergoing surgery or invasive procedures where the checklist is applied.
sources.clinical_setting: Operating room or procedural setting before anesthesia, before incision, and before leaving the room.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for checklist documentation of identity, site, procedure, consent, anesthesia equipment checks, pulse oximetry, allergy, airway or aspiration risk, blood loss, team communication, procedure record, counts, specimens, equipment issues, and recovery concerns. It does not replace local UAE policy.

exact_sections.heading: Before induction of anesthesia — Sign In
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation of identity, site, procedure, consent, site marking, anesthesia machine and medicine check, pulse oximetry, allergy, difficult airway or aspiration risk, and anticipated blood loss.

exact_sections.heading: Before skin incision — Time Out
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports team confirmation and documented critical concerns before incision; medicine-timing items are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Before patient leaves operating room — Sign Out
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented procedure name, counts, specimen labeling, equipment problems, and key recovery or management concerns without generating a plan.
